claim: Official reason in the letter and in the conversation with HR — "by my own choice" / "moving to be with family".
quote: In the letter it's "by my own choice," and in the conversation with HR I said "moving to be with family," everyone writes that kind of thing.

claim: The real reason diverges from the official one: there's no move, the phrasing is convenient so as not to stir up conflict on the way out.
quote: The real story is there's no move at all, I'm staying in the same city, it's just... a convenient phrasing so I don't stir up drama on the way out.

claim: Final formulation of the real reason: expertise devalued by a management decision.
quote: Because my expertise got devalued by a management decision

claim: Point of no return — a specific day, May 14th, an all-hands meeting where the position was reorganized into a "multifunctional" role with partial procurement duties.
quote: It was May 14th, we had an all-hands meeting, and Dmitry announced that my position was being reorganized into a "multifunctional" role, like now I'd also be handling procurement part-time.

claim: After this meeting the decision to leave became irreversible.
quote: I realized there's no going back, I was literally sitting in the meeting room thinking — okay, that's it, this is definitely over

claim: Trigger event — three days after the meeting, on May 17th, the reagent budget was cut by 40% without warning, learned about it from the storekeeper, messaged a friend about a job that same evening.
quote: literally three days after the meeting, May 17th, they cut my reagent budget by 40 percent with no warning, I found out about it from the storekeeper, not from my manager, and that's when I messaged a friend at MedGenom that same evening, asked if they had any openings.

claim: Active job search began the evening of May 17th after the trigger, but there were earlier isolated applications to openings in February.
quote: I applied to a couple of openings back in February, if I'm honest, just half-heartedly, just in case.

claim: A week after reaching out to the friend there was an interview, the offer came through in June.
quote: within a week there was an interview, the offer came through in June.

claim: Leaving for MedGenom for a lead specialist position on NGS panels.
quote: To MedGenom, it's a private lab, it'll be a lead specialist position for NGS panels.

claim: Reason for choosing that place: pay about 30% higher and a two-year approved budget for the project.
quote: At MedGenom the pay is noticeably higher, around thirty percent, and more importantly, there's a scientific lead for the new division, I talked to him, they already have budget approved two years out for a specific project.

claim: Held back by the team — three people willing to work with her on any project — and by habit.
quote: The team. We really had a strong team, three people I'd go into any project with. And also, no point hiding it, habit — I've been here since the lab first opened, it was hard to picture myself somewhere else.

claim: Also held back by her own project — the rare hereditary disease panel.
quote: the rare hereditary disease panel was mine, I poured my heart into it, it was hard to think about dropping it halfway through.

claim: In April told the department head Ira directly that if this kept up she wouldn't be able to work; felt her expertise was being used and discarded.
quote: in April I went to Ira, the department head, and told her straight up — look, if this keeps up, I won't be able to work, I feel like they're just using my expertise and then tossing it aside.

claim: Ira promised to escalate the issue, but nothing actually changed.
quote: She said "I understand, I'll take it up the chain," and she sort of did, but nothing actually changed, two weeks later they told me "well, just keep running the current protocols for now."

claim: Back in November raised uneven workload distribution in the group chat; the manager's reaction was just an emoji and a generic phrase with no action.
quote: Dmitry replied with an emoji and something like "we value your engagement," and that was it, the topic never came up again.

claim: In January talked to the manager one-on-one about burnout; his response was "tough out the quarter".
quote: Once, in January, I went into his office, told him I felt burned out from constantly putting out fires instead of doing steady, methodical work. He said everyone's like that right now, just need to tough out the quarter.

claim: COMPENSATION/GROWTH/MANAGEMENT — mixed category: managerial devaluation of expertise plus higher pay and predictability at the new place.
quote: Both, honestly I don't know which one tipped the scale, probably predictability in the end. Though the money is nice too, no denying it.

claim: PREVENTABLE — management knew in advance (November, January, April), but there was no response.
quote: three times, November, January, April — I wasn't heard

claim: Retention signal: not a one-off concession, but a durable reprioritization of the budget — that would have been enough to stay.
quote: What mattered to me wasn't a one-off concession, but knowing it would stay that way going forward.

claim: Sign of a systemic pattern: colleague Katya is also considering options for similar reasons (uneven workload distribution).
quote: Katya is definitely considering options, but I don't think it's directly because of me, she's got her own similar reasons, the workload distribution hits everyone the same way.

claim: Publicly the tone is loyal/neutral (mentions moving), personally in response to a direct question — neutral, without disclosing details, but with a specific formulation of the core conflict.
quote: I'll be honest but neutral. I won't badmouth anyone, I'll just say we didn't see eye to eye on the management approach to priorities.

claim: Main insight: leaving because expertise was devalued by a management decision, irreversibility hit on May 14th, preventable with a timely response to the April, January, and November signals.
quote: the point of no return came on May 14th at that meeting, and the trigger was the reagent budget cut on May 17th

claim: Respondent first says things were "more or less fine" until March, then admits she had already complained back in November and January — the interviewer points out the inconsistency directly, respondent admits the formulation was imprecise.
quote: Well... yeah, I guess I wasn't quite accurate there. If I'm being completely honest, it had been building for a long time, March was just the point where I stopped believing anything would change, not the point where it all started.
